name: Earth Day
description: The environmental movement, Earth Day's origins, and related ecology facts

questions.q: On what date is Earth Day observed each year?

source.notes: Chosen to fall between spring break and final exams.

questions.q: In what year was the first Earth Day held?

source.notes: April 22, 1970, with rallies across the United States.

questions.q: Approximately what percentage of Earth's surface is covered by oceans?

source.notes: About 361 million km² of a total ~510 million km² surface.

questions.q: What image is the universal symbol for recycling?

source.notes: Designed by Gary Anderson in 1970 for a Container Corp. contest.

questions.q: Which gas do plants absorb from the atmosphere during photosynthesis?

source.notes: Plants release oxygen as a byproduct.

questions.q: Which is the largest tropical rainforest in the world?

source.notes: Covers about 5.5 million km² across nine South American countries.

questions.q: What term describes the variety of plant and animal life in a particular habitat?

source.notes: Term popularized by biologist E.O. Wilson.

questions.q: Which U.S. senator is considered the founder of Earth Day?

source.notes: Wisconsin Democrat; awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1995.

questions.q: Which U.S. federal agency was created in December 1970, the same year as the first Earth Day?

source.notes: Established by executive order under President Nixon.

questions.q: Which landmark climate agreement was opened for signatures on Earth Day, April 22, 2016?

source.notes: 175 parties signed at UN headquarters in New York that day.

questions.q: The first Earth Day was scheduled for April 22 because it fell between which two events on American college campuses?
choices: Start of fall semester and midterms

source.notes: Organizers wanted maximum student participation.

questions.q: Who served as the national coordinator of the first Earth Day in 1970 and later led Earth Day Network?

source.notes: Young Stanford graduate recruited by Gaylord Nelson.

questions.q: Which sitting U.S. president signed the Clean Air Act of 1970 and created the EPA later that year?

source.notes: The bipartisan environmental agenda of the early 1970s.

questions.q: What is the most common type of debris polluting the world's oceans?

source.notes: Estimated 8+ million metric tons enter oceans annually.

questions.q: What is the leading direct driver of tropical deforestation worldwide?

source.notes: Includes cattle, soy, palm oil, and smallholder subsistence farming.

questions.q: Which international organization maintains the Red List assessing species extinction risk?

source.notes: International Union for Conservation of Nature, founded 1948.

questions.q: Which animal is widely cited as the world's most trafficked mammal?

source.notes: Poached for scales used in traditional medicine and meat.

questions.q: Which potent greenhouse gas — produced by livestock, wetlands, and landfills — has far more warming potential per molecule than CO2?

source.notes: About 80× stronger than CO2 over a 20-year horizon.

questions.q: Approximately how many people participated in the first U.S. Earth Day in 1970?

source.notes: About 10% of the U.S. population at the time.

questions.q: Which 1969 environmental disaster off the California coast is widely credited with inspiring Senator Nelson's push for Earth Day?

source.notes: January-February 1969; third-largest U.S. oil spill at the time.

questions.q: Roughly what percentage of Earth's total water supply is freshwater?

source.notes: And only ~1% of that is easily accessible — most is locked in glaciers and ice caps.

questions.q: What is the primary food source of the giant panda?

source.notes: Pandas spend 10-16 hours a day eating; bamboo makes up 99% of their diet.
